[[Levity]] and **consoling** are adjacent but structurally different responses to distress: [[levity]] deflects pain by refusing to engage with it directly; **consoling** acknowledges the pain and addresses it honestly. In the social world of *The Great Gatsby*, [[levity]] frequently performs the function of **consoling** — shallowly, without genuine engagement.

a firmly held belief or opinion not easily abandoned. A **consoling** thought is sometimes one held without genuine [[conviction]] — adopted provisionally, because it is needed, because the alternative is too bleak to face without it. Fitzgerald's characters are expert at telling themselves **consoling** things they do not truly believe: the [[conviction]] is absent, but the emotional need is present.

in a manner marked by longing or gentle melancholy; with pensive, tender sadness. Where [[wistfully]] describes the *manner* in which a person looks, speaks, or acts — the outward expression of inward yearning — **consoling** describes the quality of something directed *against* that yearning: the attempt to relieve it. A character may gaze [[wistfully]] across the water, needing a **consoling** word that never comes.

giving courage, confidence, or renewed hope; inspiring in a forward-looking way. *Heartening* is future-directed — it promises that things will improve or that effort is worthwhile. **Consoling** is present-directed — it works with the loss or disappointment that has already occurred, seeking to make it bearable rather than promising its reversal. If *heartening* says 'it will be better', **consoling** says 'I understand that it hurts'.

idioms.explanation: to find comfort or compensation in something after a loss or disappointment; to use something as a source of emotional relief: *he consoled himself with the thought that he had done everything he could; she consoled herself with wine and music and the company of people who asked no difficult questions.* In *The Great Gatsby*, virtually every character consoles themselves with something: Gatsby with his belief in the repeatability of the past; Nick with the role of dispassionate observer; Daisy with money, beauty, and the careless ease of those who never have to account for the damage they leave behind.

relatedForms.description: to comfort someone in a time of grief, distress, or disappointment; to say or do something that alleviates sorrow or makes a painful situation more bearable: *to console a grieving friend; he could find nothing to console her; she consoles herself by walking.* The root verb from which **consoling** (adjective / present participle), *consoled* (past tense), *consolation* (noun), and *consolatory* (adjective) all derive.

the comfort received after a loss or disappointment; a thing that makes a difficult situation feel slightly better: *he found little consolation in the result; a small consolation; it was some consolation to know she had not suffered.* Most common in the set phrase *consolation prize* (a prize given to the runner-up or loser, acknowledging effort without conceding victory) and in the construction *take consolation in* (to draw comfort from something despite adversity).

intended to console; having the purpose or effect of providing comfort: *a consolatory letter; consolatory words; a consolatory smile.* The more formal and slightly archaic adjectival synonym of **consoling** in its attributive function; found chiefly in written and literary contexts where the full Latin weight of the word is wanted.

not able to be consoled or comforted; overwhelmed by grief or distress to a degree that no **consoling** word or gesture can reach: *the child was inconsolable; she was inconsolable for weeks.* The negative prefix *in-* inverts the possibility of **consoling**: if **consoling** names the successful reach toward relief, *inconsolable* names the state in which that reach cannot succeed.

wordHistory: **Consoling** is the present participle and adjectival form of *console*, which enters English in the 16th century from French *consoler*, itself from Latin *consolari* — to comfort, to solace, to relieve grief. Latin *consolari* is a compound of the intensifying prefix *con-* (together, thoroughly) + *solari* (to console, to comfort, to soothe). The root *solari* is related to *solace* (comfort, relief) — borrowed into English in the 13th century via Old French *solas* — and to the Latin noun *solamen* (a means of comfort, a consolation). The *sol-* root in this family carries no connection to *sol* (the sun); it derives from a Proto-Indo-European base associated with relief and appeasement. The noun *consolation* arrived in English before the verb, appearing in the 14th century via Old French *consolacion*. The adjectival use of **consoling** — describing a quality rather than an action — becomes increasingly common in the 18th and 19th centuries as English prose develops more precise vocabulary for emotional and psychological states. By Fitzgerald's era the word sits comfortably in literary prose as the precise term for something that brings relief from disappointment or grief — with a slight suggestion that such relief is partial, provisional, and perhaps illusory: a **consoling** thought is not a solution but a temporary shelter.
contextStory: Nick had grown practised at finding **consoling** interpretations. After that last night at the mansion, he told himself **consoling** things about what Gatsby had believed in — that the belief itself had been the point, that wanting something with that degree of intensity was, in its own way, a kind of achievement. The **consoling** version of the story was the one he kept returning to on the drive back to West Egg: that Gatsby had been all right at the end, that the dream had been worthy of the dreamer. It was **consoling** to believe this. He was not entirely sure he believed it. But on a night like that, with the bay quiet and the green light gone dark, a **consoling** story was the only kind worth telling.

definitions.text: a firmly held belief or opinion, especially one that a person is not easily persuaded to abandon; a deep personal certainty about something moral, religious, or philosophical: *she had come to her convictions slowly, and she held them with the tenacity of someone who has paid for each one; a man of strong political convictions.*
examples: *He acted not from expedience but from conviction — the rare and unsettling kind that makes a person appear slightly mad in a drawing room.*
*Her convictions about the old money world were not shaken by argument; they had been formed long before argument was possible, and they lived below the reach of reason.*

definitions.text: the quality of being certain; the appearance or feeling of being genuinely persuaded; firmness and sincerity in voice, manner, or expression — as opposed to performance or pretence: *his voice lacked conviction; she spoke with such conviction that no one thought to question her.*
examples: *The speech had all the right words but none of the conviction — it was the kind of address a man gives when he knows he is expected to say something and has nothing left to believe.*
*Gatsby smiled with conviction. It was one of those rare smiles with a quality of eternal reassurance in it, that you may come across four or five times in life.*

definitions.text: a formal declaration by a court or jury that a person is guilty of a criminal offence; the state or fact of having been found guilty and sentenced: *a prior conviction for fraud; a criminal conviction carries consequences that persist long after the sentence is served; the conviction was overturned on appeal.*
examples: *His conviction had been for bootlegging, though the charge sheet preferred the more clinical language of tax evasion and conspiracy.*
*A conviction, once recorded, shadows a man wherever he goes — into ballrooms as much as boardrooms, in a world where reputation is everything and proof is rarely required.*

mainExamples: *"Can't repeat the past?" he cried incredulously. "Why of course you can!"* — F. Scott Fitzgerald, *The Great Gatsby*, Chapter 6. (Gatsby's reply to Nick carries the absolute **conviction** of a man who has built five years of planning on a single certainty — a conviction so total it has ceased to distinguish itself from fact.)
*She spoke with **conviction** about the life she imagined for herself — not the life she had, but the one she deserved, the one that had somehow been misaddressed and delivered to someone else.*

*A prior **conviction** for fraud would have ended any other man's social ambitions; in West Egg, where no one asked the right questions, it was merely another rumour among many, and rumours, Gatsby had learned, could be managed with a party and a smile.*
usageNote: **Conviction** operates in three distinct registers that rarely overlap. In personal and moral contexts (senses 1 and 2), **conviction** is a mark of integrity and seriousness: *a person of convictions* is admirable; *to speak with conviction* is to be persuasive and genuine. In legal contexts (sense 3), the word is entirely neutral and technical, denoting a court's formal finding of guilt; *a prior conviction* carries no moral commentary — it is a fact of record. The contrast matters in *The Great Gatsby*, where the two registers collide: Gatsby's moral **convictions** (his absolute belief in the possibility of recapturing the past) and his probable criminal **convictions** (the bootlegging, the fixed World Series, the bond fraud) occupy the same man and the same word. Note that *conviction* in sense 2 — the quality of sounding convinced — can be **feigned**, which is precisely what Nick learns to watch for in the novel: the brilliant performance of **conviction** by people who believe nothing at all. The Fitzgerald line in the main examples is a verified direct quotation from Chapter 6; it illustrates sense 2 (Gatsby's absolute personal certainty). The surrounding examples are illustrative.

comparisons.description: having or showing a composed, serious manner worthy of respect. **Conviction** and [[dignified]] bearing are both marks of a certain kind of social authority, but they come from different sources. **Conviction** is internal — it is what a person believes, the depth of their certainty, the sincerity behind their expression. [[Dignified]] bearing is external — it is how a person appears, the composure they present to the room. In *The Great Gatsby*, old money grants [[dignified]] ease almost automatically; **conviction** of Gatsby's kind — raw, urgent, not quite concealed — is precisely what marks him as an outsider. One can be [[dignified]] without believing anything at all.

formally granted or bestowed (a title, degree, or honour) upon someone by an institution or authority. **Conviction** is self-generated — it grows from within, from experience, from desire, from the particular shape of a person's need. What is [[conferred]] comes from outside — a credential, a status, a right formally transferred. Gatsby has **conviction** in abundance and almost nothing [[conferred]] upon him by legitimate institutions (his Oxford credential is partial and [[feigned]]). Daisy has everything [[conferred]] — breeding, status, the ease of inherited wealth — and her **convictions**, such as they are, dissolve the moment they require anything of her.

pretended; simulated; not genuine. The deepest irony of sense 2 of **conviction** is that it can be [[feigned]]: a person can *speak with conviction* — project absolute certainty, warmth, sincerity — and mean nothing by it. Tom Buchanan speaks with **conviction** about racial theory and the carelessness of the rich; Daisy's voice carries **conviction** of a particular musical kind. Nick gradually learns to hear the difference between genuine **conviction** and the [[feigned]] kind. Gatsby alone, Nick concludes, seems to have possessed something that could not be entirely [[feigned]] — though even this conclusion is held with less than total **conviction** by the end of the novel.

a striking disclosure of something previously unknown or hidden. A [[revelation]] undoes certainty — it arrives as new information that reorganises what one thought one knew. **Conviction** resists [[revelation]]: the deeply convicted person is not easily moved by new evidence. Gatsby's **conviction** that the past can be repeated makes him almost immune to [[revelation]] until the very end; the [[revelation]] of Daisy's true nature — that her voice is full of money, that she will not come — arrives not gradually but all at once, and his **conviction** cannot survive it.

the quality of being easily influenced by the opinions, feelings, or suggestions of others; susceptibility to impression. **Conviction** and [[impressionability]] are near opposites on a single axis of belief. The person of strong **conviction** is anchored — opinions arrive and deflect off them; the person of high [[impressionability]] is fluid — opinions arrive and reshape them. Nick's self-described quality of [[impressionability]] is precisely what makes him a perfect narrator: he can be moved, can receive impressions, can feel the force of Gatsby's **conviction** without being entirely captured by it. Gatsby himself has almost zero [[impressionability]] — his **conviction** is so total that five years of Daisy's absence have not dented it by a millimetre.

an acceptance that something is true or real; a firmly held opinion or principle. *Belief* is the broader, more neutral term: one has beliefs about almost everything, trivial and important. **Conviction** implies intensity, commitment, and often moral weight — a **conviction** is a belief that has been tested, confirmed, and held against pressure. One might *believe* it will rain; one does not have a *conviction* about it. In *The Great Gatsby*, the word Fitzgerald reaches for when describing Gatsby's certainty about Daisy is always stronger than mere belief — it is **conviction** in the fullest sense, the certainty that organises an entire life.

the quality of being sure about something; the state of being free from doubt. *Certainty* describes an epistemic condition — the absence of doubt. **Conviction** is warmer and more active: it implies not just the absence of doubt but the presence of deep personal investment in the belief. One can have *certainty* about a mathematical proof; *conviction* requires something that matters to the person who holds it. Gatsby's certainty that he can repeat the past is also a **conviction** — it drives action, shapes behaviour, defines the man.

idioms.explanation: to express an opinion or belief in a manner that communicates genuine certainty and sincerity, making it difficult for the listener to doubt or dismiss what is being said. The phrase emphasises the *quality* of expression rather than the truth of the content — one can speak with conviction and be entirely wrong, as Gatsby's monologue about the repeatable past demonstrates.

of an argument, voice, or person: to be genuinely persuasive; to have the quality of sounding truly believed. *His story didn't quite carry conviction* — there was something too rehearsed about the details, a smoothness that came from repetition rather than truth. The phrase is often used in the negative, precisely because it is the absence of this quality that is easier to detect.

in legal usage: a criminal conviction recorded before the current offence or proceeding; part of a person's criminal history. In the social world of *The Great Gatsby*, the phrase takes on wider resonance: almost everyone carries *prior convictions* in the broader sense — beliefs formed before the present moment that govern behaviour without ever being examined again.

relatedForms.description: to formally find (a person) guilty of a criminal offence after trial: *the jury convicted him on all three counts; she was convicted of fraud and sentenced to five years.* The legal sense of **conviction** (sense 3) is the noun formed directly from this verb — a **conviction** is the outcome of having been *convicted*.

a person who has been found guilty of a crime and is serving a sentence, especially one confined in prison: *the convict's file showed three prior offences.* The noun is strictly legal; it does not share the moral or personal-belief senses of **conviction**.

to cause (a person) to believe something firmly; to persuade someone of the truth or validity of something: *nothing could convince him that the plan had failed; she convinced herself that he would return.* **Conviction** (sense 1) is the state of having been convinced — the settled result of a persuasion, whether by argument, experience, or desire. Gatsby *convinced himself* so thoroughly that the word *conviction* barely captures the totality of his belief.

firmly persuaded; having no doubt: *she was convinced he was lying; I am convinced this is the right course.* The adjective is the adjectival form of *convince*, standing adjacent to **conviction** (sense 1): to be *convinced* is to hold a **conviction**, though *convinced* is slightly weaker and more momentary — one can be *convinced* for the afternoon; a true **conviction** lasts a lifetime.

not persuasive; failing to carry conviction; not sounding or appearing genuine: *his alibi was unconvincing; the performance was technically accomplished but unconvincing.* The word maps onto sense 2 of **conviction** — the quality a thing *lacks* when it does not carry conviction. Much of Tom Buchanan's rhetoric in the novel is, on examination, deeply *unconvincing*, though in the room it is delivered with the confidence of a man who has never needed to be convincing to be believed.

wordHistory: From Latin *convictio* (a proving, a refuting), itself from *convincere* — a compound of *com-* (together, thoroughly) + *vincere* (to conquer, overcome, defeat). *Vincere* is the root of a cluster of English words about winning and proving: *victory*, *victor*, *invincible* (unable to be conquered), *vanquish* (through Old French), and — less obviously — *convince* and *convict*, both of which derive from the idea of overcoming a person's resistance to a truth, either by defeating their doubts in argument or by defeating their denial in court. The Proto-Indo-European root *weik-* (to fight, to conquer) underlies *vincere* and connects it distantly with Old English *wīg* (battle, war) and with the name *Victor*. In classical Latin, *convincere* meant to prove conclusively, to refute, to demonstrate beyond doubt — it carried the idea of intellectual conquest, of an argument that leaves no room for further resistance. The English word *conviction* entered the language in the 15th century in its legal sense (the formal proof of guilt in a court) and acquired its personal-belief sense (the state of being thoroughly persuaded, of having one's doubts conquered) by the 16th century. The sense of *quality of expression* — speaking with **conviction**, lacking **conviction** — develops naturally from the personal-belief sense: a person who holds a belief so firmly that their doubts have been fully conquered will naturally speak in a manner that reflects that conquest.
contextStory: Gatsby spoke with absolute **conviction** — not the **conviction** of a man who has considered the alternatives and chosen carefully, but the **conviction** of a man for whom the alternatives have simply ceased to exist. Nick had met people who held opinions with great force, but Gatsby's **conviction** was of a different order entirely: it was structural, load-bearing, the single pillar on which the whole elaborate architecture of his life rested. Remove the **conviction** that the past could be repeated, that Daisy was still reachable, that five years were a minor administrative error rather than an irreversible fact — remove that, and the mansion, the parties, the pink suit, the shirts tumbling from the balcony, the green light at the end of the dock — all of it would simply cease to make sense.

definitions.text: the standards of behaviour or moral conduct that a society considers acceptable and proper; the norms of treating others with dignity and respect, without which a person is not considered decent: *to observe the decencies; stripped of the last decencies of civilised life.*
examples: *The fundamental decencies are not rules of etiquette but something deeper: a sense that the other person has inherent worth that cannot be bargained away.*
*He could perform the outward decencies — the handshake, the measured condolence, the appropriate expression — but the inner reality behind those gestures was simply absent.*

definitions.text: the things and conditions considered necessary for a dignified human existence; the minimum comforts, privacy, and respect without which life lacks basic dignity: *the decencies of a private room; denied the decencies most people take for granted.*
examples: *The overcrowded tenements allowed for none of the decencies — no privacy, no quiet, no corner one could call one's own.*
*She wanted for her children not wealth but only the simplest decencies: a clean room, regular meals, the right to sleep without fear.*

mainExamples: *"...a sense of the fundamental **decencies** is parcelled out unequally at birth."* — F. Scott Fitzgerald, *The Great Gatsby*, Chapter 1. (Nick quotes his father's advice — the sentence that frames the entire novel's moral logic: that decency is congenital, distributed arbitrarily, and not equally, which explains everything Nick will witness that summer.)

*Tom possessed the inherited vocabulary of the **decencies** — he could name them, perform them on social occasions, wear their costume — but the actual moral substance they were supposed to represent appeared to have been parcelled to him in a very small measure at birth.*

*What distinguished Gatsby from the Buchanans, Nick concluded, was not money or manners but exactly this: Gatsby, for all his fabrications, retained some instinctive respect for the **decencies** that Tom and Daisy, careless in their ease, had long since ceased to notice.*
usageNote: **Decencies** (plural) occupies a more specific and literary register than the uncountable singular *decency*. When one says *a man of decency*, the singular refers to an abstract moral quality — an overall character trait. When Fitzgerald writes *the fundamental **decencies***, he reaches for the plural because he means not a general virtue but a set of distinct moral dispositions — specific forms of regard for others — that can, in his formulation, be parcelled out selectively and unequally, as if they were discrete items in a limited supply. The plural forces concreteness: these are real, enumerable requirements of decent human behaviour, not a single vague virtue. **Decencies** is most common in three constructions: *the fundamental / basic / common decencies* (the essential requirements); *observe / maintain the decencies* (perform the required minimum); and *the decencies of life / civilised existence* (the conditions necessary for human dignity). Distinguish carefully from *proprieties* (the accepted conventions of social behaviour — rules of etiquette, questions of what is appropriate in a given setting) and from *civilities* (the surface forms of polite exchange — greetings, courtesies, small formal gestures). **Decencies** run deeper than both: they concern not how one behaves in company but whether one treats other people as beings worthy of moral consideration at all. The word [[parcelled]] in Fitzgerald's sentence is essential to its argument: it implies passive distribution by some indifferent agency, a finite supply given out at birth — as though moral character were not cultivated but allocated, and the allocation were permanently unequal.

comparisons.description: divided and distributed in portions; allocated in shares. In Fitzgerald's sentence, **decencies** and [[parcelled]] are inseparable: the noun names what is distributed, and the verb supplies the mechanism — passive, arbitrary, permanent. [[Parcelled]] out *unequally* carries the sting: there is a finite stock of moral sensibility, and not everyone receives an equal measure. The verb transforms a statement about decency into a statement about fate.

a firmly held belief or moral certainty; the quality of expressing genuine persuasion. **Decencies** are outward-facing — they concern how one treats *others*; [[conviction]] is inward-facing — it concerns what one believes *oneself*. One can hold powerful [[conviction|convictions]] and still violate every decency: Tom Buchanan is certain of his own rightness and contemptuous of others' dignity simultaneously. The two qualities can coexist or be entirely severed from each other.

having or showing a composed, serious, self-respecting manner. [[Dignified]] describes demeanour — the outward presentation of self-worth. **Decencies** describe how one treats *others* — the outward presentation of respect for their worth. The distinction matters in Fitzgerald's world: Tom is often [[dignified]] in bearing and entirely without the fundamental **decencies** in practice. The two qualities are not the same and do not imply each other.

feeling or showing haughty contempt for others; behaving as though one is superior and others unworthy of serious consideration. [[Supercilious]] is in some sense the direct negation of **decencies**: to be [[supercilious]] is to deny others the basic moral regard that the **decencies** require one to extend. In the novel, the most [[supercilious]] characters — those most invested in their own superiority — are precisely those to whom the fundamental **decencies** appear to have been most sparingly allotted.

relating to or characteristic of a father; fatherly in tone or manner. The statement about **decencies** in the novel is specifically [[paternal]] advice — Nick's father's words, transmitted through memory and framing the entire narrative. The [[paternal]] origin of the idea is significant: it is not a doctrine Nick arrived at himself but a moral instrument handed to him by the previous generation, the lens through which he will measure everyone he meets that summer.

conformity to the accepted standards of social behaviour; behaviour that is correct and appropriate in a given context. *Propriety* is a matter of social convention — what is fitting, what is expected, what the occasion demands. **Decencies** are deeper: they concern not whether behaviour is socially appropriate but whether it reflects a genuine recognition of other people's worth. One can satisfy every *propriety* while violating every decency — which is precisely the social achievement of old-money carelessness in Fitzgerald's novel.

polite and courteous behaviour in social interactions; the surface forms of respectful exchange. *Civility* is the minimum level of courtesy — holding a door, acknowledging a greeting, not being openly rude. **Decencies** go further: they are not about courtesy but about the moral recognition that underlies it. One can be civil and hollow; the **decencies** Fitzgerald's father describes are not performances of politeness but something congenital and either present or absent — the capacity to feel, rather than merely display, regard for others.

idioms.explanation: to conform to the expected standards of decent behaviour in a social situation, even when genuine feeling is absent: *they no longer liked each other, but they continued to observe the decencies at table — saying please, passing the salt, not raising their voices.* The phrase emphasises the performative, minimal nature of the norm — one observes the decencies as a floor, not a ceiling.

the minimum conditions considered necessary for a dignified human existence: adequate shelter, privacy, food, safety, and basic respect. *He had worked his whole life to provide his family with at least the decencies of life* — not luxury, but the baseline below which existence loses its dignity.

relatedForms.description: the quality of conforming to moral and social standards of proper conduct; the state of being decent. When used as an uncountable noun, *decency* refers to the abstract virtue as a whole: *a man of decency; have the decency to apologise; common decency demands it.* **Decencies** (plural) concretises this abstraction into specific, enumerable norms or requirements — the individual forms that decency takes in practice.

conforming to accepted standards of propriety or morality; of an acceptable standard or quality: *a decent man; a decent wage; decent living conditions.* The adjective from which the noun chain *decency → **decencies*** derives. **Decencies** are, at their most basic, the conditions or behaviours that make a situation or person *decent* in the eyes of others.

in a manner that conforms to accepted standards of propriety or adequacy: *treated decently; paid decently; he behaved as decently as the circumstances allowed.* The adverb operates across both senses of *decent*: moral decency (*treated decently* — with proper regard) and adequate quality (*paid decently* — sufficiently).

behaviour that violates accepted standards of propriety, especially in a sexual context; something shockingly offensive or morally improper: *charged with indecency; an act of gross indecency.* The antonym of *decency* in its stronger register. A violation of **decencies** is not always an *indecency*, but *indecency* always represents a violation of **decencies** in their most basic form.

wordHistory: From Latin *decentia* (seemliness, propriety, becoming behaviour), a noun formed from *decens* (fitting, seemly, proper), the present participle of *decere* (to be fitting, to suit, to become). *Decere* belongs to the Proto-Indo-European root *dek-* (to take, to accept, to seem good or fitting), which also gives Latin *decorum* (that which is fitting; propriety), *dignus* (worthy — source of *dignity*, *dignified*), and *docere* (to teach — source of *doctor*, *doctrine*, *document*). The family of ideas connects what is fitting with what is taught and accepted: the *decent* thing is, at its root, what one *takes* to be right, what society accepts as appropriate. English *decency* is recorded from the 16th century, first in the sense of seemliness and modesty in appearance and behaviour, then broadening to encompass moral standards of conduct. The plural **decencies** — concretising the abstract virtue into specific enumerable norms — appears from the 17th century onward and becomes particularly useful in the kind of moral-philosophical discourse that Fitzgerald's father's advice represents: not virtue in the abstract, but a set of specific moral dispositions, distributed by birth, that either orient a person toward others' dignity or do not.
contextStory: His father had said it once, and Nick had never forgotten. The **decencies**, the old man explained, were not things you learned — they were given out at birth, unevenly, like good eyesight or perfect pitch. Some people simply received more of them. That summer on Long Island, Nick watched the theory prove itself without being asked. He watched Tom Buchanan pivot from a woman's death to dinner — the interval between catastrophe and appetite no longer than it takes to change a shirt — and understood at last what his father had meant. The **decencies** were not manners. They were not knowing which fork to use. They were something quieter and more essential: the capacity to stop, when a person is gone, and allow it to mean something. Gatsby, for all his invented self, seemed to possess more of these **decencies** than anyone in East Egg. It was one of the many things Nick could not entirely explain.

definitions.text: having or showing a composed, serious manner that commands respect; carrying oneself with calm gravity and self-possession: *a dignified bearing; a dignified silence; a dignified response to provocation.*
examples: *She accepted the news with a dignified composure that silenced the room.*
*His dignified refusal to argue was more devastating than any counterattack could have been.*

definitions.text: past tense and past participle of *dignify* — to make something appear worthy, important, or deserving of honour; often used ironically, to confer an undeserved status or grand name on something: *to dignify a rumour by repeating it; dignified under the name of scholarship.*
examples: *"that flabby impressionability which is dignified under the name of the 'creative temperament'" — F. Scott Fitzgerald, The Great Gatsby, Chapter 1*
*I refuse to dignify that question with an answer.*

definitions.text: to add distinction or honour to something; to elevate or ennoble by association or by the act of naming: *the ceremony dignified the occasion; the presence of the poet dignified the event.*
examples: *The simple meal was dignified by the care with which it had been prepared.*
*He had a gift for dignifying the ordinary — making the everyday seem worth attending to.*

mainExamples: *"that flabby impressionability which is dignified under the name of the 'creative temperament'" — F. Scott Fitzgerald, The Great Gatsby, Chapter 1*
*She maintained a dignified silence throughout the hearing — neither defending herself nor condemning others.*
*To call it a philosophy would be to dignify it with more coherence than it possessed.*
usageNote: Fitzgerald's deployment of **dignified** in Chapter 1 is richly ironic. The standard meaning of *dignified* — bearing or conferring genuine dignity — is here twisted: the 'creative temperament' has not *earned* dignity, it has merely been *given* a dignified name, a grand label that conceals the [[flabby]] reality beneath. The construction *X is dignified under the name of Y* is a formal passive construction that highlights the gap between the glamorous name and the unworthy thing it covers. This use of **dignified** (as past tense of *dignify*) is relatively rare in modern speech — more common is the adjectival sense (a dignified person, manner, or occasion). But the verb's ironic register is potent: to say something has been *dignified* in this way is to accuse it of masquerading. Note the fixed phrase *I won't dignify that with a response*, which operates on the same logic — the speaker refuses to confer even the dignity of a reply.

comparisons.description: raised to a higher level, morally, intellectually, or socially. *Elevated* is more neutral and often genuine — an elevated tone, an elevated position; **dignified** (in the ironic Fitzgerald sense) suggests a title conferred on something that has not earned it.

made noble; raised in rank or moral standing. *Ennobled* tends to suggest genuine transformation; **dignified** in its ironic use implies surface elevation only — a grand name with nothing to match it beneath.

simulated or pretended rather than genuine. Where [[feigned]] describes a personal performance of false emotion, **dignified** (in Fitzgerald's sense) describes a social performance: the [[flabby]] thing has been given a grand name and allowed to pose as something worthy.

lightness and lack of seriousness. **Dignified** is its conceptual opposite — it implies gravity, decorum, and the appearance of worthiness. But Fitzgerald shows that **dignified** can be hollow: gravity of name without substance of character.

the word Fitzgerald places alongside **dignified** in the same sentence. The contrast is the engine of the irony: the thing is [[flabby]]; the name given to it is **dignified**. The gap between the two words is where Fitzgerald's contempt lives.

the specific quality that has been **dignified** in Fitzgerald's sentence — dressed up as the creative temperament when it is, in his view, merely [[flabby]] [[impressionability]]. **Dignified** is the act; [[impressionability]] is what has been elevated beyond its actual worth.

idioms.phrase: I won't dignify that with a response
idioms.explanation: a fixed phrase used to refuse to reply to a question, accusation, or provocation — implying that responding would confer undeserved credibility on it: *When they accused him of cowardice, he simply said he would not dignify the charge with a response and walked away.*

relatedForms.description: to make worthy of honour or respect; to confer distinction upon something; often used ironically, to give a grand name to something undeserving: *to dignify a pretence with the word 'principle'; the ceremony was meant to dignify the occasion.*
the quality of being worthy of honour, respect, or esteem; composed self-respect and gravity of bearing: *personal dignity; the dignity of the occasion; beneath one's dignity.*
a person holding a high rank or office; someone invested with the outward signs of dignity: *a visiting dignitary; foreign dignitaries at the state dinner.*
lacking dignity; embarrassingly unseemly or inappropriate to the occasion: *an undignified scramble for the exit; an undignified remark.*

wordHistory: From *dignify* (15th century), from Old French *dignifier*, from Late Latin *dignificare* (to consider worthy, to honour) — composed of *dignus* (worthy, fitting) + *facere* (to make, to do). *Dignus* is from Proto-Indo-European *dek-* (to take, to accept), also the root of *decent* (fitting), *decorum* (what is fitting), *deign* (to condescend to do), and *disdain* (to consider unworthy — *dis-* + *dignus*). *Facere* gives the enormous family including *fact*, *affair*, *manufacture*, *perfect*, and *effect*. The sense of **dignified** as *made to appear worthy* — the ironic sense Fitzgerald exploits — is implicit in the Latin root: *dignificare* already contains the idea of *making* something worthy, whether or not it is so in itself.
contextStory: The committee had been meeting for three hours to decide what to call the new initiative. Several names had been proposed and rejected. Finally someone suggested a phrase so vague and grand that it could mean almost anything — and the room exhaled in collective relief. The scheme had been **dignified** at last. Nothing about the plan itself had changed: the funding was inadequate, the timeline was impossible, the objectives were unclear. But now it had a name, and the name was **dignified**, and in that boardroom, in that organisation, a **dignified** name had always been considered the first step toward becoming the thing it described. Or so everyone told themselves, as they filed out into the corridor with their folders and their **dignified** expressions.

definitions.text: having or displaying qualities traditionally regarded as feminine — softness, delicacy, over-refinement of manner — in a way felt to be unbecoming in a man; lacking masculine vigour or directness: *an effeminate voice; effeminate manners; a faintly effeminate fastidiousness about his surroundings.*
examples: *His voice was a shade too high, his gestures a shade too graceful — enough to be noticed but not enough to be named, a quality that hung about him like expensive scent.*
*The young man struck her as effeminate in his fastidiousness — the way he recoiled from the mud, the way he held his cuffs clear of the table.*

definitions.text: in the extended figurative sense: weakened by excessive comfort or refinement; having lost firmness of character through over-indulgence in pleasure or luxury; insufficiently vigorous: *an effeminate civilization; the effeminate luxury of the age.*
examples: *The critic saw in their prose style the symptoms of an effeminate age: too much refinement, too little willingness to face an ugly fact plainly.*
*His philosophy had an effeminate quality — all sensitivity, no structure; all feeling, no conclusion.*

mainExamples: *He was one of those men who looked as though they had always been a little too comfortable — a certain **effeminate** softness about the jaw, the hands, the way he received rather than offered ideas.*
*His voice had an **effeminate** quality — not weak exactly, but over-careful, as though every syllable had been arranged in advance and set out like silverware.*

*Meyer Wolfsheim's cufflinks, his pink suit, his studied sentimentality — Fitzgerald clothes his underworld in an **effeminate** delicacy that makes the violence beneath all the more unnerving.* — F. Scott Fitzgerald, *The Great Gatsby*.
usageNote: **Effeminate** belongs to a formal and literary register and carries considerable historical weight. In classical rhetoric — from Aristotle through the Puritans — "effeminacy" denoted a moral and civic failing: the man who had surrendered to pleasure and lost his readiness for public duty. Fitzgerald deploys the word not in that abstract sense but as a precise visual and acoustic marker — gesture too smooth, voice too polished, presence too decorative. In contemporary usage the word has become sensitive: it is frequently perceived as derogatory toward men with non-normative gender expression. In literary analysis it remains the standard term when an author is deliberately exploiting its weight. In everyday speech it has been largely displaced by neutral alternatives: *delicate*, *refined*, *gentle*, or the gender-neutral *graceful*. Note that **effeminate** describes manner and quality, not identity; in precise usage it should not be applied as a synonym for gay or feminine-presenting.

comparisons.description: haughtily disdainful; behaving as though one considers others inferior. Both **effeminate** and [[supercilious]] are signals Fitzgerald uses to mark a character's social and moral distance from ordinary reality. [[Supercilious]] is a posture directed outward and upward — contempt as proof of superiority. **Effeminate** is a posture directed inward — excessive self-decoration as a form of avoidance. Both register as failures of authentic engagement, but by different routes.

thin and graceful in build. [[Slender]] is a neutral physical description carrying no moral charge; **effeminate** is applied to manner, voice, gesture — expressive qualities coded as belonging to a particular gender. A man may be [[slender]] without the least suggestion of **effeminacy**, and conversely, **effeminate** manners may belong to a man of heavy build. The two words occupy different registers: physical versus behavioural.

of a voice: low-pitched and slightly rough or hoarse; of a person: physically large and strong. [[Husky]] is in many respects the direct counterpoint to **effeminate**: where **effeminate** describes thinness, over-refinement, and excessive smoothness, [[husky]] implies depth, mass, and a pleasing roughness. In prose the contrast between voices or physiques is often drawn along exactly this axis — the **effeminate** tenor against the [[husky]] baritone.

with tender longing or melancholy. [[Wistfully]] describes an emotional quality; **effeminate** describes a quality of manner and presentation. In Fitzgerald's prose the two often overlap: a character whose **effeminate** refinement signals an inability to engage roughly with the world tends also to look at that world [[wistfully]] — longing for a reality to which he is too fastidious to belong directly.

fine and subtle in structure or quality; needing careful handling. *Delicate* is free of gender charge — a delicate mechanism, a delicate operation, a delicate constitution. **Effeminate** specifically invokes gender norms and carries an evaluative loading that *delicate* does not. One who is **effeminate** may also be *delicate*, but the choice of **effeminate** signals something about perceived gender deviation that *delicate* deliberately avoids.

ostentatious elegance; the air of confident, expensive style. [[Swank]] and **effeminate** overlap in the territory of excess — too much ornamentation, too evident a care for appearances. But [[swank]] describes a lifestyle and the display of wealth; **effeminate** describes a gender-coded softness in character or manner. The pink suit may be [[swank]]; the way it is adjusted in the mirror may be **effeminate**.

lacking firmness; soft and yielding — physically or morally. In their figurative senses, [[flabby]] and **effeminate** converge: both describe the loss of rigour and strength. But **effeminate** carries a gendered metaphor (assimilation to the feminine), while [[flabby]] carries a physiological one (muscle that has lost tone). A [[flabby]] argument is imprecise and under-developed; an **effeminate** one is over-refined and insufficiently direct.

relatedForms.description: the quality or state of being effeminate; the possession of characteristics traditionally coded as feminine in a degree felt to be excessive or unbecoming in a man: *he was teased for his effeminacy; the effeminacy of his manners struck the older men as a failure of will.* Historically, *effeminacy* was treated as a moral and civic failing — the mark of a citizen absorbed in pleasure and unfit for public virtue.

in an effeminate manner; with over-refined delicacy of manner or movement: *he dressed effeminately for the period; she noted how effeminately he declined the rougher activities of the weekend.* The adverb is rarer than the adjective and tends to appear in descriptive or analytical prose rather than dialogue.

an archaic verbal use meaning to make effeminate; to weaken or soften by excess of ease or luxury; to deprive of masculine character: *luxury effeminates the character.* No longer in productive use; the verbal sense has been entirely absorbed by the adjective and noun.

wordHistory: From Latin *effeminatus*, past participle of *effeminare*: to make feminine, to deprive of masculine qualities. The verb is formed from *ex-* (out of, away from) + *femina* (woman) — literally "to draw out of the masculine into the feminine". *Femina* is related to the root *felare* (to suckle) and traces back to the Indo-European root *dhe-* (to suck, to nurse); the same root appears in Latin *felix* (fertile, fortunate, happy) and in English *female* and *feminine*. In classical Latin *effeminare* carried a strong moral censure: the citizen who yielded to pleasure was held to be *effeminatus* — unfit for war, governance, or public life. The word entered English in the 14th and 15th centuries via Old French *effeminé*. Puritan and Renaissance writers used it extensively in moral tracts, treating effeminacy as a threat to the body politic. By the 19th century the word had narrowed toward description of a particular person's manner or appearance rather than the moral condition of a civilization. In the 20th century its connotations shifted further: it increasingly carried associations with male homosexuality or non-normative gender expression, though the original sense was entirely about civic and moral weakness, not sexual orientation. Fitzgerald uses it in its older, appearance-based sense — as a precise visual marker of a character's relationship to masculinity, class, and self-presentation.
contextStory: He arrived at the party in a white linen blazer with an expression of unruffled condescension that Nick found, obscurely, irritating. His voice was **effeminate** in the precise sense of being over-managed — each syllable considered, the vowels slightly prolonged, the whole delivery suggesting a man who had never needed to raise his voice to be heard. There was nothing overtly **effeminate** about him in the way people meant when they intended an insult; it was more the quality of the surfaces — each phrase polished, each gesture arranged, each lapel exactly so. Nick had the feeling that this man had spent his life in rooms with controlled climates and had never worn anything twice. That **effeminacy** — not weakness exactly, but a principled refusal of roughness — was, in the end, what made him difficult to trust.

definitions.text: plural: discrete instances or episodes of great happiness, exhilaration, and proud triumph; moments in which the spirit is lifted sharply above its ordinary state — not a sustained condition but a series of peaks, each temporary and structurally incomplete: *the short-winded elations of men; brief elations followed by long silences; elations that never hardened into lasting joy.*
examples: *"…the abortive sorrows and short-winded **elations** of men."* — F. Scott Fitzgerald, *The Great Gatsby*, Chapter 1.
*The **elations** of that summer — unexpected, intense, and brief — left him unprepared for the autumn that followed.*

definitions.text: in the singular, *elation* — a feeling of great happiness and exhilaration; an elevated, exultant state of mind produced by success, good fortune, or intense pleasure; related to but more restrained than *euphoria*, and typically anchored in a real and proportionate cause: *she felt a sudden elation when the results were announced; the elation of a first success.*
examples: *The elation of finishing the novel lasted exactly one evening — by morning the next problem had arrived.*
*There is an elation peculiar to dangerous crossings — a lightness that arrives only when the danger is past and the far bank is underfoot.*

mainExamples: *"No — Gatsby turned out all right at the end; it is what preyed on Gatsby, what foul dust floated in the wake of his dreams that temporarily closed out my interest in the [[abortive]] sorrows and short-winded **elations** of men."* — F. Scott Fitzgerald, *The Great Gatsby*, Chapter 1.
*He had learned not to trust his **elations** — they came quickly, announced themselves brightly, and then dissolved before he had time to act on them.*

*The novel's opening paragraph sets up a world of failed feeling: [[abortive]] sorrows and [[short-winded]] **elations**, human emotional life measured against some implied standard of completeness and found wanting.*
usageNote: Fitzgerald's choice of the plural **elations** in Chapter 1 of *The Great Gatsby* is a deliberate and precise one. The singular *elation* would name a state — something sustained, a condition one inhabits. The plural **elations** turns it into a series of discrete episodes, each one arriving and departing, each one as briefly lit and structurally inadequate as the last. The word sits alongside "[[abortive]] sorrows" in the same clause, building a parallel portrait of human emotional life as doubly truncated: grief that fails to complete itself, and happiness that fails to sustain itself. Together, under the pressure of Nick's retrospective narration, they define ordinary men as beings incapable of the full measure of feeling that Gatsby — whatever his faults — did achieve. In contemporary English, *elation* (singular) is the common form, used as the name for any peak emotional state: *she felt elation; the elation of winning*. The plural **elations** is rarer and carries a literary flavour, implying a pattern of repeated brief highs rather than one continuous feeling. The word is occasionally misread as purely positive; in Fitzgerald's construction it is slightly elegiac — these are **elations** found insufficient, measured and dismissed.

comparisons.description: quickly exhausted; lacking stamina or breath to sustain an effort or an emotion. [[Short-winded]] is the adjective Fitzgerald attaches directly to **elations** in Chapter 1 — the phrase *[[short-winded]] **elations*** is the key unit of meaning. **Elations** names the experience; [[short-winded]] renders the verdict on its duration, finding it insufficient.

an intense state of happiness and well-being, often disproportionate to circumstances and potentially symptomatic of a manic or chemically altered state. *Euphoria* is more extreme than **elation** and carries a stronger suggestion of unreliability or excess — a detachment from proportionate cause. **Elation** is more anchored, more measured, and can be an entirely appropriate response to genuine good fortune.

a feeling of great excitement, happiness, and energy — the closest common synonym of **elation** but with a stronger emphasis on the bodily and kinetic dimension of being stirred and animated. *Exhilaration* often has a physical quality (the exhilaration of speed, of cold air, of altitude); **elation** is more inward and more closely tied to triumph or vindication.

lightness of spirit; the quality of treating things with a becoming lack of seriousness or weight. **Elations** are peaks — intense, upward surges of feeling with a specific and identifiable cause. [[Levity]] is a maintained surface quality, a habitual refusal of heaviness that does not require an external occasion. [[Levity]] can coexist with unhappiness; **elation** cannot.

failing to reach completion; cut short before it can fully develop or achieve its aim. In the same Fitzgerald clause, [[abortive]] is applied to *sorrows* while **elations** receives *[[short-winded]]*. The symmetry is exact and devastating: human grief is [[abortive]] — it cannot fully form; human joy is [[short-winded]] — it cannot sustain itself. Together they frame a dismissal of ordinary emotional life as fundamentally insufficient at both its poles.

a quality of gentle, reflective sadness mingled with longing; a pensive, tender ache directed toward what one cannot have or return to. **Elations** and [[wistfulness]] represent the two poles of desire in the novel: **elations** are the promised peaks — the glittering arrivals of joy — while [[wistfulness]] is the emotional register of everything that leads up to those peaks and falls away from them. Gatsby's entire project in East Egg is an attempt to convert [[wistfulness]] into **elation**; the novel's tragedy is that the conversion is never quite complete.

damaged or spoiled — but still present, still existing as an achieved result. **Elations** that are [[short-winded]] dissolve before they can be consolidated; something [[marred]] was achieved and then damaged. The distinction matters in the novel: Gatsby's parties were [[marred]] by the absence of the one person they were designed to attract; his **elations** when he finally stood with Daisy were [[short-winded]] for a related reason — the reality could not quite sustain the weight of the dream.

relatedForms.description: the singular form — a feeling of great happiness, pride, and exhilaration; an exalted state of mind: *she felt elation at the news; the elation of winning; the sudden elation that follows unexpected good fortune.* The base form from which **elations** is the literary plural.

experiencing or showing elation; overjoyed and exhilarated; lifted above one's ordinary emotional state by success or good news: *he was elated by the result; she looked elated, almost dazed by the news.*

to make very happy or proud; to cause to feel elation; to lift the spirits of: *the victory elated the whole team; she was elated by the unexpected praise.* Somewhat formal; in speech, *delight* or *thrill* are more common.
causing a feeling of elation; uplifting and exhilarating in a vivid and immediate way: *an elating discovery; an elating piece of news; the elating quality of the first warm day.*

wordHistory: From Latin *elatio* (a lifting up, exaltation, pride) — the noun formed from *elatus*, past participle of *efferre* (to carry out, to lift up, to elevate, to raise), composed of *ex-* (out, upward) + *ferre* (to carry, to bear). *Ferre* is one of the most productive roots in both Latin and English, generating *fertile*, *transfer*, *infer*, *refer*, *confer*, *suffer*, *offer*, *defer*, *prefer*, and dozens more. *Elatio* in classical Latin could mean both physical elevation and mental exaltation — the same vertical metaphor (being borne upward) governs both uses. In late Latin and scholastic usage, *elatio animi* (lifting of the spirit) was a recognised psychological and spiritual concept — sometimes positive (genuine joy proportionate to its cause) and sometimes pejorative (pride, self-inflation, the sin of presumption). English borrowed *elation* directly from the Latin noun in the 16th century, initially in contexts of spiritual and emotional uplift; the word gradually settled into its modern, secular meaning of intense happiness. The plural **elations** — treating moments of high feeling as countable, discrete episodes — appears predominantly in Romantic and post-Romantic literary writing on the inner life. Fitzgerald's use of **elations** in *The Great Gatsby* is among the most precisely deployed instances of the word in American fiction: by pluralising and then qualifying the word with *short-winded*, he converts what might have been a celebration of feeling into an elegy for its inadequacy.
contextStory: The **elations** came without warning — and that was exactly what made them both wonderful and treacherous. One afternoon, crossing the bridge on his way home, he felt it: a sudden lift, almost physical, as though the city had briefly consented to become something magnificent. He did not know what caused it. He stood at the railing for a moment and let it move through him. By evening the feeling was gone, leaving only the ordinary streets and the ordinary worries. These were the **elations** Nick recognised — abrupt, groundless, [[short-winded]], gone before they could be named. Fitzgerald was right to put them in the plural. They were not one feeling but many: a series of brief risings, each one failing to carry its own weight. The **elations** of men, Nick thought. Brief, bright, and unable to sustain themselves.

definitions.text: free from an obligation, duty, rule, or condition that applies to others; not subject to a requirement that is otherwise general: *exempt from military service; a tax-exempt organisation; the organisation was declared exempt from the new reporting requirements.*
examples: *"Only Gatsby, the man who gives his name to this book, was exempt from my reaction — Gatsby, who represented everything for which I have an unaffected scorn."* — F. Scott Fitzgerald, *The Great Gatsby*, Chapter 1.
*Certain categories of income are exempt from taxation in most jurisdictions, though the categories vary considerably by country.*

definitions.text: to free someone or something from an obligation, liability, or rule that would otherwise apply; to grant a formal exclusion from a general category or requirement: *the court exempted him from jury duty; Congress exempted small businesses from the new regulation.*
examples: *The board voted to exempt long-serving employees from the new attendance policy, reasoning that their record spoke for itself.*
*No experience can be entirely exempted from consequence — not even the most charming life.*

mainExamples: *"Only Gatsby, the man who gives his name to this book, was **exempt** from my reaction — Gatsby, who represented everything for which I have an unaffected scorn."* — F. Scott Fitzgerald, *The Great Gatsby*, Chapter 1.
*He seemed to believe himself exempt from the rules that constrained everyone else — and, for a while at least, the world seemed to agree with him.*
*Being charming is not the same as being exempt from consequence — though Gatsby was charming enough to make the distinction invisible for years.*
usageNote: **Exempt** carries a quasi-legal flavour even in figurative use: it implies that the standard rule exists and applies to everyone else, and that the exemption is a *deliberate deviation* — either formally granted by authority or simply observed and acknowledged. This is precisely the weight Fitzgerald gives the word. Nick states that Gatsby *represents everything for which he has unaffected scorn*, and in the very same sentence exempts him from that scorn: the word registers a conscious act of exclusion, not a failure to notice. That single word does a great deal of moral work. Grammatically: in the adjectival sense **exempt** governs *from* (*exempt from duty*, *exempt from the rule*); in the verbal sense the construction is *exempt someone from something*. **Exempt** is more formal and precise than *free from* or *not subject to* — prefer it in legal, philosophical, and literary contexts where the idea of being *taken out of a category* matters, as opposed to simply lacking a quality.

comparisons.description: admitted to private knowledge or to something kept from others; sharing in a secret. [[Privy]] describes a person who *has access to* something restricted; **exempt** describes a person who is *released from* something generally applied. Gatsby is both [[privy]] to the machinery of the very rich and **exempt** from the moral accounting Nick applies to them — the two conditions reinforce each other.

behaving as if superior to others; disdainfully dismissive. The [[supercilious]] person appoints themselves above the rules by attitude; the **exempt** person is released from a rule by the genuine acknowledgement of another. Tom is [[supercilious]] — he acts as if the rules do not touch him. Gatsby is **exempt** — Nick actually grants him the exclusion, however reluctantly and without fully understanding why.

damaged, spoiled, impaired — present, but flawed. Where **exempt** describes freedom from a judgment or rule, [[marred]] describes the damage left by it. In Chapter 1, Gatsby alone is **exempt** from Nick's corrosive reaction to the East Egg world: he alone is not [[marred]] by Nick's disenchantment, not tainted by it.

in the manner of a snob; with a consciousness of social superiority. [[Snobbishly]] describes the manner of one's own self-elevation through contempt for others; **exempt** describes the status granted by another's judgment. Nick confesses he reacts [[snobbishly]] to almost everything about East Egg society — which makes it the more striking that Gatsby alone emerges **exempt** from that reaction.

a firmly held belief or view; absolute certainty in one's own position. [[Conviction]] is the internal force that drives a person; **exempt** describes external release from an obligation. Gatsby's [[conviction]] — his absolute belief in the dream — is perhaps what makes him **exempt** in Nick's eyes: a man of such complete belief inhabits a different order of being and cannot be judged by the same standard.

protected against a disease, influence, or obligation, often inherently or by nature rather than by formal rule. *Immune* suggests a built-in resistance requiring no external grant; **exempt** suggests a deliberate, quasi-formal exclusion from a category. Gatsby is not *immune* to Nick's judgment — he is **exempt** from it, which implies a conscious act of exclusion on Nick's part, however instinctive.

not looked for or asked for; arriving without being pursued. [[Unsought]] describes the manner in which something comes; **exempt** describes the status of being released. Nick's attachment to Gatsby is partly [[unsought]] — it formed without intention; his exemption of Gatsby from his usual reactions is similarly unreasoned — not argued into, simply found to be so.

idioms.explanation: used to emphasise that a universal rule, experience, or condition applies to everyone without exception: *No one is exempt from loss — it comes to all of us in time; the new policy makes clear that no department is exempt.*

relatedForms.description: the state of being exempt; a formal release from an obligation, duty, or rule; an instance of being taken out of a general category: *a tax exemption; he applied for a medical exemption; no exemption was granted.* Both the act and the resulting status.

granted freedom from an obligation or rule by an authority: *He was exempted from the exam on medical grounds; the charity was exempted from the new reporting requirements.* Also used adjectivally: *the exempted organisations were listed in an appendix.*

not exempted from a particular obligation, rule, or liability; subject to the standard requirement. In US labour law specifically: describing employees who are *not* excluded from the overtime and minimum-wage provisions of the Fair Labor Standards Act — *non-exempt employees are entitled to overtime pay at one-and-a-half times their regular rate.*

a distant relative of **exempt** through the Latin root *eximere* (to take out, remove): an *example* was literally something *taken out* of the general run as a specimen or illustration. The shared root *emere* (to take, buy) also connects **exempt** with *preempt*, *redeem*, and *peremptory*.

wordHistory: From Latin *exemptus*, past participle of *eximere* — to take out, free, remove — composed of *ex-* (out of, away from) and *emere* (to take, buy), from Proto-Indo-European *em-* (to take, distribute). The same root *emere* gives English *example* (literally: something taken out of the general run as a specimen), *preempt* (to take beforehand), *redeem* (to buy back), and *peremptory* (admitting no contradiction; literally, taking away any possibility of reply). In Latin, *exemptus* described primarily a legal state: being taken out from under the operation of a law or obligation. The word passed into Old French as *exempt*, retaining the legal sense, and entered English in the 15th century in legal and administrative documents before broadening into general usage. By Fitzgerald's era **exempt** had long settled into a double register: literal (tax-exempt, exempt from military service, exempt from liability) and figurative (exempt from a judgment, from a reaction, from a moral category). It is the figurative register that Fitzgerald deploys with surgical precision: Gatsby is *taken out* of Nick's general reaction to the East Egg world — removed from the category into which everyone else falls — which is exactly the word's Latin meaning made philosophical.
contextStory: The house on the Sound ran on an unspoken code: no one arrived without being weighed, and no one left without a verdict. Old money judged new money; new money judged those who had none. Only one man seemed, in some way that no one could quite explain, to be **exempt**. He appeared at the door with a smile the other guests found difficult to account for, and the usual whispered assessments simply did not attach to him. After his death, someone remarked that it was perhaps the smile itself that had kept him **exempt** — it told each person that they were understood and forgiven. A person who feels understood rarely finds the will to judge; and so, without quite knowing it, they had voted him **exempt** from the verdict they passed on everyone else.

definitions.text: a thing, especially a building or man-made structure, that is ugly, unsightly, or offensive to the eye, particularly when it stands in otherwise attractive or respectable surroundings: *the derelict factory was considered an eyesore by everyone in the neighbourhood; they finally demolished the eyesore that had blighted the seafront for thirty years.*
examples: *A small **eyesore** — peeling paint, a sagging gutter, a letterbox listing to one side — says something very different from a grand one: it says that the owner has not quite arrived, or is no longer trying.*
*The billboard rose above the valley like a personal insult — garish, enormous, and entirely indifferent to the landscape it had turned into an **eyesore**.*

definitions.context: noun (figurative — anything conspicuously ugly or out of place)
definitions.text: anything — a garment, a piece of furniture, a social situation, a person's behaviour — that strikes the eye or sensibility as intolerably jarring, crude, or aesthetically wrong in its context: *his suit was an eyesore among the dinner jackets; the conversation had become an eyesore — loud, graceless, impossible to ignore.*
examples: *Among the white clapboard houses and clipped hedges, the half-built extension stood as a glaring **eyesore** — a reminder that money had arrived but taste had not.*
*The Valley of Ashes was, in its totality, an **eyesore** the size of a county — grey, heaped, industrial, and entirely without apology.*

mainExamples: *"My own house was an **eyesore**, but it was a small **eyesore**, and it had been overlooked, so I had a view of the water, a partial view of my neighbor's lawn, and the consoling proximity of millionaires — all for eighty dollars a month."* — F. Scott Fitzgerald, *The Great Gatsby*, Chapter 1. (Nick's self-deprecating acknowledgment is a masterclass in ironic understatement: the house is an **eyesore** — that is stated plainly — but the word is immediately qualified and then quietly abandoned as Nick lists what the **eyesore** gives him access to. The admission of ugliness becomes the basis of a kind of modest pride.)

*The difference between Nick's modest **eyesore** and Gatsby's grotesque mansion was not the difference between ugly and beautiful — it was the difference between an **eyesore** that knew its place and an **eyesore** that did not.*

*Every great estate on the Sound had its corresponding **eyesore** somewhere out of frame — the service entrance, the storage shed, the gardener's cottage falling to pieces behind the topiary. Beauty of that scale requires an **eyesore** to maintain it.*
usageNote: **Eyesore** is a compound noun (eye + sore) that works as a blunt, informal verdict on ugliness — particularly the ugliness of the built environment. It carries the implication not merely of ugliness in the abstract but of ugliness that intrudes on what ought to be attractive: an **eyesore** is offensive precisely because it stands out against something better. In *The Great Gatsby*, Nick deploys the word with characteristic self-awareness: he applies it to his own house and then immediately demonstrates — with inventory-like precision — why the **eyesore** does not bother him. The register is informal to colloquial and is most naturally used of buildings, billboards, abandoned structures, and neglected lots. In formal architectural or planning contexts, a near-synonym would be *blot on the landscape* or *visual intrusion*, both of which carry the same sense of something that disfigures what surrounds it. **Eyesore** almost always requires an article or possessive (*an eyesore*, *a complete eyesore*, *the local eyesore*) rather than appearing bare.

comparisons.description: damaged or spoiled in appearance; having beauty or quality impaired by something unwanted. **Eyesore** and [[marred]] both describe the intrusion of ugliness into an otherwise acceptable scene, but they differ in grammatical shape and emphasis. [[Marred]] is a verb and adjective: it describes the *effect* of something ugly on something that was better without it — the landscape is [[marred]] by the billboard. **Eyesore** is a noun: it names the *thing itself* as the offending object — the billboard is an **eyesore**. Nick's house is an **eyesore** in its own right; the Valley of Ashes [[marred]]s the geography between the Eggs and the city. Together they form the novel's persistent vocabulary of the visually wrong.

on the surface only; lacking depth; of or relating to appearance rather than substance. **Eyesore** and [[superficial]] sit on opposite sides of the novel's preoccupation with surfaces and appearances. The [[superficial]] world — Daisy's voice, Gatsby's shirts, the green light — is the one that glitters and seduces. **Eyesore** is what the [[superficial]] world excludes and denies: Nick's house, the Valley of Ashes, the billboard eyes of Doctor T. J. Eckleburg. A society organised entirely by [[superficial]] values produces **eyesores** wherever it has not bothered to manage appearances — or wherever it has run out of money to do so.

the enclosed yard around a barn; by extension, anything rustic, unrefined, or deliberately deflated to domestic and unglamorous scale. **Eyesore** and [[barnyard]] are both instruments of Fitzgerald's ironic deflation, but they operate on different registers. [[Barnyard]] is ironic in tone — it applies a deliberately humble, domestic frame to something grand (Long Island Sound becomes a wet [[barnyard]]). **Eyesore** is more direct — it simply names something as ugly. Nick's house is an **eyesore**; Long Island Sound is a [[barnyard]]. The two images together establish, in the novel's opening geography, that Nick's corner of this world is the unglamorous one — which is, paradoxically, what makes him trustworthy.

a thing that spoils or damages a place, a person, or a situation; something that causes decay or deterioration. *Blight* is more severe and more systemic than **eyesore**: a *blight* affects an area over time, causes deterioration or decay, and is harder to remove — it is the rot beneath the surface. An **eyesore** is primarily a visual offence; a *blight* implies deeper, more lasting damage. The Valley of Ashes is both an **eyesore** and a *blight*: its ugliness is visible from the road, but its damage to the people who live and work there — the Wilsons among them — is structural and irreversible.

something outrageously or grotesquely ugly, especially a building or structure of shockingly bad design. A *monstrosity* is an **eyesore** at maximum intensity: larger, more extreme, more actively offensive to taste. Both words are informal verdicts on ugliness, but *monstrosity* implies that the thing has transgressed beyond mere ugliness into a kind of aggressive aesthetic crime. Gatsby's mansion — that factual imitation of a French château — is closer to a *monstrosity* than an **eyesore**: it is large enough and ornate enough to demand attention, which is precisely what an **eyesore** that knows its place, like Nick's cottage, does not do.

idioms.explanation: a mild, colloquial way of acknowledging that something is noticeably ugly without being aggressively condemnatory about it: *the new car park is a bit of an eyesore, but at least there's somewhere to park.* The qualifier *a bit of* softens the judgment and is typical of British understatement; Nick's equivalent move in *The Great Gatsby* is to call his house *a small eyesore* — acknowledging the fault while immediately limiting its significance.

relatedForms.description: causing physical pain or discomfort; tender to the touch; by extension, painful or distressing. The second element of **eyesore** — *sore* — preserves an older sense of the word in which *sore* meant painful, grievous, or distressing: an **eyesore** is literally something that is *sore* (painful, grievous) *to the eye*. This sense of *sore* survives in idioms such as *a sore point* (a sensitive or painful topic) and *a sight for sore eyes* (something or someone very welcome to see) — the latter phrase standing in ironic counterpoint to **eyesore**.

fatigue or discomfort affecting the eyes, caused by prolonged use, poor light, or excessive focus on a screen. **Eyestrain** is a literal medical compound (eye + strain); **eyesore** is a figurative one (eye + sore). Both words build on the conceit of the eye as capable of suffering — of being hurt by what it is made to look at. **Eyestrain** takes this literally; **eyesore** takes it metaphorically.

unpleasant or ugly to look at; offending the eye. The closest single-word synonym of **eyesore** in adjective form: *unsightly* describes the quality that makes something an **eyesore**. *An unsightly building* = *a building that is an eyesore*. **Unsightly** is slightly more formal and is common in planning documents, newspaper architecture criticism, and official complaints: *the committee described the proposed development as unsightly and out of keeping with the surrounding area.*

wordHistory: **Eyesore** is a compound of two Old English words: *ēage* (eye — from Proto-Germanic *augōn*, cognate with German *Auge*, Dutch *oog*, and ultimately related to Latin *oculus* and Greek *ōps*) and *sār* (pain, wound, grief, distress — the ancestor of modern *sore*, cognate with German *sehr*, meaning *very*, which has retained the sense of intensity while losing the specifically painful connotation). The compound *ēage-sār* — literally *an eye-pain* or *a grief to the eye* — appears in Middle English and describes something that causes the eye distress by its ugliness or offensiveness. The metaphor is physiological: looking at something ugly is, in this framing, a form of sensory suffering, like pressing on a bruise. The word has been in continuous use since the 14th century with remarkably little change in meaning or register: it has always been informal, colloquial, and pointed — a blunt lay verdict that bypasses the vocabulary of formal aesthetics. Fitzgerald's use of it in 1925 to describe Nick's cottage is thus a deliberate choice of register: Nick could have said *modest*, *unprepossessing*, *undistinguished*, or *plain*. He chooses **eyesore** — the word of a man who looks at his own house without illusion and without embarrassment.
contextStory: Nick had made his peace with the **eyesore** early. Other men might have hung curtains, planted a hedge, angled the door away from the road. Nick simply accepted that his cottage was an **eyesore** — small, overlooked, the sort of thing that [[marred]] a listing in an estate agent's window — and got on with appreciating what the **eyesore** had purchased him: the water, the light, the green dock across the bay. It occurred to him later that Gatsby's mansion was also an **eyesore**, in its way — vast and restless and impossible to look away from, the kind of **eyesore** that refuses to be overlooked and is worse for it.

forms: base: **feign**; feigns, feigning; past tense / past participle: **feigned**

definitions.text: not genuine; simulated or performed for effect; assumed deliberately to conceal what one actually feels or to achieve a social end: *feigned indifference; feigned surprise; a smile of feigned warmth that did not reach the eyes.*
examples: *Her feigned enthusiasm was convincing to everyone in the room except the one person who knew her well enough to notice the effort behind it.*
*The feigned innocence on his face was so perfect that even Nick, who suspected everything, nearly believed it.*

definitions.text: pretended to feel or be in a certain state; simulated an emotion, condition, or quality one does not actually possess in order to deflect, deceive, or protect oneself: *he feigned sleep when he heard her approach; she feigned interest in the conversation.*
examples: *"Frequently I have feigned sleep, preoccupation, or a hostile levity when I realized by some unmistakable sign that an intimate revelation was quivering on the horizon."* — F. Scott Fitzgerald, *The Great Gatsby*, Chapter 1.
*He feigned disinterest in Daisy's voice, but his hands, pressed flat against the table, gave him away.*

mainExamples: *"Frequently I have **feigned** sleep, [[preoccupation]], or a hostile [[levity]] when I realized by some unmistakable sign that an intimate [[revelation]] was quivering on the horizon."* — F. Scott Fitzgerald, *The Great Gatsby*, Chapter 1.
*The **feigned** surprise of the very rich is its own art form: they have seen everything, expected most of it, and learned long ago to perform delight at the appropriate moment.*
*He had **feigned** so many emotions for so long that he was no longer certain which of them, if any, had ever been real.*
usageNote: **Feigned** belongs to a formal and literary register. In ordinary conversation, speakers tend to prefer *faked* or *pretended*, both more colloquial and less weighted. What **feigned** adds is a suggestion of *deliberate social strategy*: the word implies not merely that something is false but that the false thing has been performed consciously, with purpose — to hold someone at a distance, to avoid an unwanted obligation, or to manage the impression one creates. This is precisely why Fitzgerald chooses it: Nick does not accidentally fail to engage; he **feigns** — a controlled, purposeful performance. The three things Nick **feigns** (sleep, [[preoccupation]], [[levity]]) form a miniature taxonomy of social armour.
